Global Hydrogen Review 2023
===========================

[Download PDF](https://iea.blob.core.windows.net/assets/ecdfc3bb-d212-4a4c-9ff7-6ce5b1e19cef/GlobalHydrogenReview2023.pdf)

![Image 25: Cover Of Global Hydrogen Review 2023 Shutterstock 1526899085](https://iea.imgix.net/8a0dc535-3ad6-4111-805a-202a0672236e/CoverofGlobalHydrogenReview2023-shutterstock_1526899085.jpg?auto=compress%2Cformat&fit=min&q=80&rect=540%2C0%2C3241%2C2160&w=1280&h=853&fit=crop&fm=jpg&q=70&auto=format)

[Overview](https://www.iea.org/reports/global-hydrogen-review-2023#overview)[Related files 1](https://www.iea.org/reports/global-hydrogen-review-2023#downloads)[Previous editions 2](https://www.iea.org/reports/global-hydrogen-review-2023#previous-editions)

#### About this report

The _Global Hydrogen Review_ is an annual publication by the International Energy Agency that tracks hydrogen production and demand worldwide, as well as progress in critical areas such as infrastructure development, trade, policy, regulation, investments and innovation.

The report is an output of the [Clean Energy Ministerial Hydrogen Initiative](https://www.cleanenergyministerial.org/initiatives-campaigns/hydrogen-initiative/) and is intended to inform energy sector stakeholders on the status and future prospects of hydrogen, while also informing discussions at the Hydrogen Energy Ministerial Meeting organised by Japan. Focusing on hydrogen’s potentially major role in meeting international energy and climate goals, the Review aims to help decision makers fine-tune strategies to attract investment and facilitate deployment of hydrogen technologies at the same time as creating demand for hydrogen and hydrogen-based fuels. It compares real-world developments with the stated ambitions of government and industry.

This year’s report includes a focus on demand creation for low-emission hydrogen. Global hydrogen use is increasing, but demand remains so far concentrated in traditional uses in refining and the chemical industry and mostly met by hydrogen produced from unabated fossil fuels. To meet climate ambitions, there is an urgent need to switch hydrogen use in existing applications to low-emission hydrogen and to expand use to new applications in heavy industry or long-distance transport.

_This report is part of the IEA’s support of the first global stocktake of the Paris Agreement, which will be finalized in the run up to COP28, the next UN Climate Change Conference, at the end of 2023. Find other reports in this series on the IEA’s_[_Global Energy Transitions Stocktake_](https://www.iea.org/topics/global-energy-transitions-stocktake)_page._

Published September 2023

### Hydrogen Production and Infrastructure Projects Database

The Database covers all projects commissioned worldwide since 2000 to produce hydrogen for energy or climate change-mitigation purposes and all projects under development worldwide of hydrogen pipelines, underground storage facilities and import/export terminals dedicated to low-emissions hydrogen and hydrogen-based fuels.

[Get data](https://www.iea.org/data-and-statistics/data-product/hydrogen-production-and-infrastructure-projects-database)

This article is part of a series on clean hydrogen.

# Chart: Which countries are leading the green hydrogen race?

![](https://img.canarymedia.com/content/uploads/Maria-Virginia-Olano.png?auto=compress%2Cformat&crop=focalpoint&fit=crop&fp-x=0.4927&fp-y=0.4011&h=50&q=80&w=50&s=4b271253c1ff0460abd847c137336912)

2 February 2024

![Two rows of large, round storage tanks labeled H2. A graphic overlay says CHART OF THE WEEK.](https://img.canarymedia.com/content/uploads/COTW-8-resized.jpg?auto=compress%2Cformat&crop=focalpoint&fit=crop&fp-x=0.5&fp-y=0.5&h=501&q=80&w=864&s=ffaf12046c145a9d5779b751a0f71e21)

Supported by

![](https://img.canarymedia.com/content/uploads/Verdagy_Logo.Horizontal.Transparent_2-color.png?auto=compress%2Cformat&fit=clip&h=60&q=80&s=7b2b66b5f058744ef7717cfc230e2593)

*Canary Media’s* [*chart of the week*](https://www.canarymedia.com/articles/columns/chart-of-the-week) *translates crucial data about the clean energy transition into a visual format.*

Whether you believe green hydrogen to be fundamental to the clean energy transition or dismiss it as an overhyped technology, one thing is for certain — more of it is coming.

But where will most of this green hydrogen be produced? Besides China’s enormous lead, the answer is a bit all over the map. According to the International Energy Agency’s [hydrogen projects database](https://www.iea.org/data-and-statistics/data-product/hydrogen-production-and-infrastructure-projects-database), these are the top 15 countries with the most green hydrogen capacity that’s operational or under construction or has secured committed financing.

![Made with Flourish](https://public.flourish.studio/resources/made_with_flourish.svg)

China is currently the [world’s largest consumer and producer of hydrogen](https://rmi.org/insight/chinas-green-hydrogen-new-era/). Most of the hydrogen it makes today is fossil-fuel-based, but the country has big plans to scale up its green hydrogen production and use the fuel to decarbonize sectors like steel and chemicals production.

Saudi Arabia is next on the list, thanks to the fact that it’s currently building the world’s largest green hydrogen project. A joint venture between ACWA Power, Air Products and Neom, the huge facility [will include](https://www.neom.com/en-us/newsroom/neom-green-hydrogen-investment) ​“up to 4 gigawatts of solar and wind energy to produce up to 600 tonnes” of green hydrogen per day. That would mean the facility will be capable of producing over 200 kilotonnes of green hydrogen per year if it operates every day. For context, the largest existing green-hydrogen facility, in China’s Xinjiang region, has a capacity of just over 44 kilotonnes per year.

![Two TV hosts, one male and one female, engage in a video chat with a reporter with light red hair, glasses and a beard.](https://img.canarymedia.com/content/uploads/pattrn-hydrogen2.png?auto=compress%2Cformat&crop=focalpoint&fit=crop&fp-x=0.1781&fp-y=0.2765&h=510&q=80&w=880&s=b152040b0a6dc57a39e6b9cf35062729)

### Video: How the Gulf Coast is leading the way on clean hydrogen production

### Should power plants burn clean hydrogen to make electricity?

### Clean energy experts break down hydrogen hype and hope

Sweden, which opened its [largest electrolyzer facility last year](https://www.hydrogeninsight.com/industrial/swedens-largest-electrolyser-project-inaugurated-to-produce-hydrogen-for-green-steelmaking/2-1-1512389), is up next, and fellow European Union members Germany and France also make the top 10. The EU has [plans](https://energy.ec.europa.eu/topics/energy-systems-integration/hydrogen_en) to ​“produce 10 million tonnes and import 10 million tonnes” of ​“renewable hydrogen” by 2030, and it has set ambitious targets to boost hydrogen use in industry and transport, positioning itself as a leader in demand-side policy incentives, which are lacking elsewhere.

The United States is just behind Sweden on green hydrogen plans. Thanks to the 2022 Inflation Reduction Act, which includes the world’s [most generous](https://www.canarymedia.com/articles/hydrogen/new-clean-hydrogen-rules-will-favor-some-regions-more-than-others) clean hydrogen subsidies, the country is expected to see a wave of new investment in green hydrogen production.

The United Kingdom, which has [its own set of support measures](https://www.gov.uk/government/news/major-boost-for-hydrogen-as-uk-unlocks-new-investment-and-jobs) for clean hydrogen, rounds out the top five countries.

In total, the world produces just 180 kilotonnes of electrolysis-based hydrogen per year right now. But that number could reach [more than 14,000 kilotonnes by 2030](https://www.canarymedia.com/articles/hydrogen/the-dawn-of-the-clean-hydrogen-economy-visualized) if all projects currently under construction become operational — and that’s not even counting the hundreds more that have been announced but don’t have investment or permitting yet.

*[Verdagy](https://www.verdagy.com/ "https://www.verdagy.com/") manufactures an advanced AWE electrolyzer system that has superior performance to almost any system in the market — high current densities and the largest membranes leading to higher hydrogen production, high efficiencies leading to lower LCOH, and wide dynamic range and fast turndowns to seamlessly integrate with renewables. In addition to its Silicon Valley factory, Verdagy operates its R&D and highly automated commercial pilot plants in Moss Landing, California, where it continues to advance its cutting-edge technology.*

[Maria Virginia Olano](https://www.canarymedia.com/about/people/maria)
is chief of staff at Canary Media.

enriched_points.expanded_content: China leads global hydrogen production, followed by Saudi Arabia's ambitious projects. Other key players include the U.S., Germany, and Australia, focusing on scaling green hydrogen capacities.
Investment in green hydrogen is surging, particularly in the U.S. due to the Inflation Reduction Act, which offers significant subsidies, boosting project developments worldwide.
Countries like Germany and the U.S. have implemented robust policies to promote hydrogen adoption, aiming for substantial production increases and integration into energy systems by 2030.
